front: Essay: defines a sensibility that 'sees everything in quotation marks,' exemplified by Tiffany lamps, Flash Gordon comics, and Bellini's operas, and described as 'good because it's awful'
Essay: uses epigrams from Oscar Wilde to describe a sensibility as 'the triumph of the epicene style'; its pure form is 'always naive'
Essay: traces Camp sensibility to Christopher Isherwood's novel The World in the Evening
Essay: opens with 'In Plato's Cave,' claiming 'to collect photographs is to collect the world'; contains 'Freak Show,' which contrasts FSA photography with Diane Arbus
Essay: calls FSA photography 'propagandistic' and criticizes Diane Arbus for her absence of beauty in the essay originally titled 'Freak Show'
Essay: title essay ends with the line 'In place of a hermeneutics, we need an erotics of art'
Essay: written while the author was being treated for breast cancer; argues against victim-blaming language around illness and against treating disease as a metaphor for personality traits
work: Illness as Metaphor (1978) and AIDS and Its Metaphors (1989)

front: Essay: contrasts the romantic glorification of tuberculosis patients with the victim-blaming directed at cancer patients; notes near-total historical amnesia about the 1918 influenza pandemic
work: Illness as Metaphor (1978) and AIDS and Its Metaphors (1989)

front: Novel: fictionalizes the lives of William Hamilton (a vulcanologist) and his wife Emma Hamilton; ends with Eleonora da Fonseca Pimentel declaring 'Damn them all'

Short Story: dialogue-heavy story about the AIDS crisis told through conversations of friends of a hospitalized man; opens with Max saying 'At first he was just losing weight, he felt only a little ill'
Author: staged a candlelit production of Waiting for Godot during the 1990s Siege of Sarajevo
Author: wrote a controversial New Yorker essay five days after 9/11 arguing 'Let's by all means grieve together. But let's not be stupid together'
Author: wrote the play Alice in Bed, featuring a tea party attended by Emily Dickinson and Margaret Fuller
Author: her son David Rieff wrote the memoir Swimming in a Sea of Death about her 2004 death
